text:
for a roadmap presentation for non-technical colleagues I need a few "reasons why" (shortform value adds) we're focusing on improving "product versions 2.0"

here's the design document
```untitled
This document is scheduled for update and verification by @Fredrik Gyllenhammar  January 1, 2025 

# Introduction

Product Versions in Otovo have historically been used to define panel requirements and classifications (Standard, Premium, Performance) for different markets. This system has served us well but is limited in scope. Product Version V2 aims to expand this concept to cover all hardware components and enable new business opportunities through hardware-specific customer journeys and campaigns.

## Background

1. **THE NEED TO ALLOW ONLY A SUBSET OF AVAILABLE PRODUCTS ON CALC PAGE:**
Frequently, when launching a new product, or trying to run a campaign about a specific brand, we run into the following need: We want to make sure that only a specific subset of our products are included in calculations on auto calculation. For example - when launching SigEnergy batteries in Norway, we wanted customers running a battery-only calc to see SigEnergy. However, Growatt was also available in Norway, and was a cheaper option. In order to ensure that customers were met by a SigEnergy calculation, we had two choices: 

1) Mute Growatt at the hardware level
2) Mute all cost models featuring Growatt on cost model level - and only have active cost models that have just SigEnergy. 

Either of these approaches would have made it impossible for a sales agent to create battery offers with Growatt. This would be a bad idea, as Growatt would be the best solution for some of the customers depending on what they already had, and it should be possible for the sales agents after conferring with the customer to offer a Growatt solution.

This is a frequently occurring problem - we want to anchor customer’s idea of price and hardware in the right place, while retaining the ability to accommodate edge cases or find a lower cost option for highly price sensitive customers. 

2. **THE ABSURDITY OF OFFERING CHOICE OF PANELS**
Today, customers can generate system price for installations with different kinds of panels. Back in the day, the look of the solar panels was regarded as customer’s main decision related to the installation. But even then - they could not pick brand, just attributes. With every new product category appearing in the home energy area, the focus on panel selection increasingly underscores the lack of choice in the other categories. Even today, for every other component, customers have a binary choice: YES/NO. So they can’t see the price estimate with different options of inverter or battery. They can’t even see what we have. This makes no sense, as these are far more important choices for the price and capabilities of the system than the panels are. It leads to customer experiences like these:

    - I want Huawei, but all I see is something called Fox
    - I want a Tesla battery, but I’m only offered the Huawei
    - I got Enphase inverter and battery, but the suggested EV-charger is …”wallbox”? Don’t they have the Enphase one? I want Enphase everything!
    
    Home energy is becoming all about eco systems - if you go Huawei on your inverter, that’s where your future battery, HEMS, backup and EV-charger will come from. If you choose Enphase, that is now your energy systems operating system - it defines your choice of other components. Suggesting a home energy system means suggesting a full package. In order to provide the customers anchoring on our product range and prices, we must make it easy to compare the price of different home energy packages - like the Huawei package or the Enphase package. 
    

These two needs can be addressed by a single solution: Changing the way “product versions” work!

Part A)  Expanding the current functionality that sorts panels into classes, to make it also sort products of all the other categories. This feature is called “product versions”, and you may recognise them in their current form as “standard”, “premium” and “performance”. In the future, it must be possible to define these packages across any number of product categories, and to do so not only by backsheet color or length of warranty, but also on the basis of brand. This will unlock:

- The ability to show prices for complete value and premium packages on calc page
- The ability to create brand based packages, such as the Tesla package or the Enphase package
- The ability to tie a campaign discount to a specific package - “10% off on Enphase systems”
- The ability to define product packages specific to the leads coming from a given partner

Part B) Give sales and operations agents the power to select hardware without regard for the product versions - meaning product versions are only enforced in customer facing automatic calculations. Sales agents would still be able to apply the, if they want, but they will have the freedom to select and combine any enabled hardware that the platform deems technically compatible in order to offer customers a system that fits their specific needs. 

# Scope

### Goals

- Extend Product Versions to cover all hardware components (panels, batteries, inverters, etc.)
- Enable hardware-specific customer journeys through product pages and iframe integration
- Provide more flexibility in package/tier definitions beyond panel classifications

**To consider**

- Redesign of the sales funnel UI/UX (only adapting existing flows)

### Non-goals

- Maintain backward compatibility with existing panel-based product versions
- Changes to the cost engine's core functionality
- Modifications to hardware compatibility rules
- Changes to the pricing/shopping cart
- Integration with manufacturer-specific inventory management
- Automated product version optimization based on market performance(?)

### Future Goals

- Support hardware-specific campaigns and brand partnerships
    - Improved campaign management system for hardware-specific promotions
- A/B testing capabilities for different product versions(?)

### Opportunities

While we’re “in the area” - here are adjacent things we might consider:

- Improve transparency and debugging capabilities for sales agents: This is a recurring problem today (*“Why can’t I find any prices?”*), where sales agents some times end up in a situation where the system can't give them any prices. There's a risk that with the new system, things are more *flexible*, leading to a higher chance of creating incompatible configurations that can't be priced. A debug tool could help them understand why they can't get a price, and what they need to do to get one.
- Transition PV calc page in Web to the new shopping cart package API used by standalone battery calc page. Product version support was one of the main blockers for not doing this straight away. This changes package creation from “create all combinations initially” to “get or create”. Fits nicely with the concept of spec-wide product versions (get or create a package for the product version chosen by the customer).
    - Related PRs: https://github.com/otovo/cloud/pull/12819 and https://github.com/otovo/cloud/pull/12935
    - This also requires thinking through [Should we maintain the concept of classifications in V2?](https://www.notion.so/Should-we-maintain-the-concept-of-classifications-in-V2-144315ebc48f80bf8a9fc99c0b98ad2d?pvs=21)

# Proposed system

The new system will:

1. Expand Product Version Model to include:
    - Full hardware specification capabilities
    - Campaign-related fields
    - Market-specific configurations
2. Modify the sales funnel to:
    - Support product version selection via query parameters
    - Present hardware packages as primary choices instead of panel classifications
    - Handle version-specific requirements throughout the process
3. Implement two possible approaches for offer creation:
    - Solution 1: Pre-filled but modifiable requirements
    - Solution 2: Optional classification with full agent control

# Release plan

1. Clean up deprecated functionality (in progress)
2. Remove product version requirement from spec builder (in progress)
3. …

# Open questions

- Which offer creation solution should we implement? (Solution 1 vs Solution 2)
- How should we handle the transition period for existing product versions?
- What level of granularity should we allow in hardware requirements?
- How do we ensure performance with potentially complex hardware requirement combinations?
- Should we maintain the concept of classifications in V2?
    - Relevant enums: `ProductClassification` and `Package` :
    
    ```python
    class Package(ChoicesEnum, metaclass=EnumMeta):
        TIER3_LARGE = ('tier3_large', 'Tier3 L')
        PREMIUM_LARGE = ('premium_large', 'Premium L')
        STANDARD_LARGE = ('standard_large', 'Standard L')
        TIER3_ECONOMIC = ('tier3_economic', 'Tier3 Eco')
        PREMIUM_ECONOMIC = ('premium_economic', 'Premium Eco')
        STANDARD_ECONOMIC = ('standard_economic', 'Standard Eco')
        TIER3_SMALL = ('tier3_small', 'Tier3 S')
        PREMIUM_SMALL = ('premium_small', 'Premium S')
        STANDARD_SMALL = ('standard_small', 'Standard S')
        CUSTOM = ('custom', 'Custom')
        STANDARD_CUSTOM = ('standard_custom', 'Standard Custom ')
        TIER3_CUSTOM = ('tier3_custom', 'Tier3 Custom')
        PREMIUM_CUSTOM = ('premium_custom', 'Premium Custom')
    
        @classmethod
        def get_for_classification(cls, classification: ProductClassification, size: PackageSize) -> Package:
            return Package(f'{classification.value}_{size.value}')
    ```
    

# Technical details

<aside>
💡 In this section, you should cover any technical details that are relevant to the design. This is only meant for developers and can be as low-level as you want.

</aside>

# Insights

Required data points to track:

- Version selection patterns
- Campaign performance metrics
- Hardware combination success rates
- Sales funnel conversion rates per version
- Debug tool usage statistics

TODO: Define specific data contracts for tracking

# Attachments

https://www.figma.com/board/y5Ow4pw1rotCGFzYqxl8nR/Product-Versions-V2?node-id=0-1&t=aCzDnICuEVfnpwm9-1
```



Give me 2 reasons.
Based on the design document, here are 2 key value-adds for improving product versions 2.0:

1. **Better Customer Experience with Complete Packages**
   - Instead of just choosing panel types, customers can see full ecosystem options (e.g., "The Huawei Package" or "The Enphase Package")
   - Makes it easier for customers to understand what they're getting and ensures compatibility across components

2. **More Flexible Campaign Management**
   - Ability to run targeted promotions for specific brands or packages (e.g., "10% off on Enphase systems")
   - Can showcase specific products for special launches while still maintaining full options for sales agents
thanks.

We're (Otovo) is using hubspot CRM that integrates with our solar platform (organizing installers, prices, generating quotes etc). We now want to integrate with a WhatsApp Business Account to send and receive messages towards customers as an alternative to SMS.

Today, our platform integrates with Twilio while Hubspot uses Sakari for SMS and a whatsapp integration.

Problems we're seeing with the HubSpot integrations are trackability and cost.

Use mermaid to draw an architecture you think is suitable for an improved, scalable system for sending and receiving SMS+Whatapp.
Here's a proposed architecture using Mermaid that centralizes messaging through Twilio for both SMS and WhatsApp, while maintaining synchronization with HubSpot:

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Otovo Platform
        A[Solar Platform]
        B[Message Service]
        C[Message DB]
    end

    subgraph External Services
        D[Twilio]
        E[HubSpot CRM]
    end

    subgraph Channels
        F[SMS]
        G[WhatsApp]
    end

    A -->|Triggers message| B
    B -->|Stores| C
    B -->|Sends via| D
    D -->|Delivers to| F
    D -->|Delivers to| G
    
    F -->|Receives| D
    G -->|Receives| D
    D -->|Webhook| B
    B -->|Sync| E
    
    B -->|Status updates| C
    E -.->|Read status| C

    style B fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

Key points of this architecture:
1. Centralized Message Service handles all communication
2. Single provider (Twilio) for both SMS and WhatsApp
3. Local message DB for tracking and analytics
4. Bi-directional sync with HubSpot
5. Webhook handling for incoming messages and status updates

This would:
- Reduce costs by using single provider
- Improve trackability through central message DB
- Maintain HubSpot integration while having more control
- Scale better for future messaging channels
